1:
"And when they were escaped, then they knew that the island was called Melita." The witnesses note the safe landing and the identification of the island. The grammatical voices note Melita (modern Malta), where the shipwrecked company came ashore. Robertson and others note the company, having escaped the sea (ch. 27), learned the name of the island only after landing.

On the escape, the witnesses note the fulfillment of God's promise (27:24): all 276 souls saved, not one lost. The deliverance from the storm and shipwreck was complete.

The puritan Henry and others note the providence: the company cast upon Malta, where they would be kindly received (v.2) and where Paul's ministry would be displayed. The verse opens the Malta episode.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the shipwrecked company, having escaped the sea, came ashore and learned the island was Melita (Malta) — the fulfillment of God's promise (27:24) that all would be saved. The verse opens the Malta episode: the deliverance complete, the company on the island. The verse marks the safe landing: all 276 souls preserved, as promised. The verse displays the providence: the company cast upon Malta, where kindness and ministry awaited. The verse displays the completion of the deliverance: the storm and shipwreck survived, the company ashore. The verse displays God's faithfulness: the promise of preservation fulfilled, not one soul lost. The verse marks the transition from the voyage to the sojourn on Malta: the company landed, soon to experience the islanders' kindness and to witness Paul's miracles. The safe landing on Malta, the fulfillment of the promise, display God's providence over the whole journey. The verse displays the deliverance that crowned the perilous voyage: all saved, the company on solid ground. The verse opens the final stage of Paul's journey to Rome: the three months on Malta (v.11) before the voyage's resumption. The verse displays the providence that, through storm and shipwreck, brought Paul safely toward Rome, where he would bear witness to Christ. The safe landing, the identification of Malta, set the scene for the kindness of the islanders (v.2), the miracle of the viper (vv.3-6), and the healings (vv.7-10). The verse marks God's faithful preservation of his servant and all with him.
2:
"And the barbarous people shewed us no little kindness: for they kindled a fire, and received us every one, because of the present rain, and because of the cold." The witnesses note the kindness of the Maltese. On "barbarous people," the grammatical voices note the term. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note "barbarians" simply means those who did not speak Greek (non-Greek-speaking), not savages — the Greek term for foreigners. The Maltese spoke a Punic dialect.

On "no little kindness," the witnesses note the humane reception. The islanders kindled a fire and received the whole company — drenched and cold from the shipwreck — with remarkable hospitality.

The witnesses note the providence and the contrast: these "barbarians" showed a humanity that shamed many who boasted of civilization. The verse displays the kindness of the Maltese to the shipwrecked.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "barbarous people" (simply non-Greek-speaking foreigners, not savages, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson) showed no little kindness — kindling a fire and receiving the whole drenched, cold company with remarkable hospitality. The verse displays the kindness of the Maltese: a humane reception of the shipwrecked. The verse marks the providence: the company received with warmth and care. The verse displays the contrast: these "barbarians" showing a humanity that shamed many who boasted of civilization. The verse displays the islanders' compassion: the fire kindled, the company received, in the rain and cold. The verse displays God's providence in the kindness: the shipwrecked company cared for by strangers. The verse displays the breadth of the hospitality: "received us every one" — all 276 welcomed. The kindness of the Maltese, the fire in the rain and cold, display the humanity that providence raised up to receive Paul and his company. The verse displays the goodness found even among the heathen: the islanders' compassion a token of the common grace of God. The verse displays the warm reception that opened the Malta sojourn: kindness that would be repaid by Paul's healings (vv.8-9). The verse marks the providence preparing the way for Paul's ministry on the island: the kindness of the people opening the door for the gospel's witness. The hospitable reception of the shipwrecked displays both human compassion and the providence of God caring for his servant.
3:
"And when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks, and laid them on the fire, there came a viper out of the heat, and fastened on his hand." The witnesses note Paul's humility and the viper. On the gathering of sticks, the grammatical voices note Paul's diligence and lowliness. The apostle, not standing on his dignity, gathered fuel for the fire — sharing the common labor.

On the viper, the witnesses note the danger. Robertson and others note the viper, roused by the heat, came out and fastened (bit, or fastened upon) Paul's hand. The serpent was venomous, its bite deadly.

The witnesses note the providence in the incident: the viper's attack would become the occasion for a display of God's power (vv.5-6). The verse displays Paul's humility and the sudden peril of the viper.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, gathering a bundle of sticks (his humility and diligence, sharing the common labor), laid them on the fire, and a viper, roused by the heat, came out and fastened on his hand (Robertson) — the venomous serpent's deadly bite. The verse displays Paul's humility: the great apostle gathering fuel like any other. The verse marks the sudden peril: the viper fastening on his hand. The verse displays the providence in the danger: the attack becoming the occasion for God's power (vv.5-6). The verse displays the lowliness of the apostle: not above the common labor, gathering sticks for the fire. The verse displays the unexpected threat: the viper, hidden in the sticks, roused by the heat, fastening on Paul. The verse displays the danger to the apostle: the deadly serpent's bite. The humility of Paul and the peril of the viper display both the character of the apostle and the providence that would turn the danger into a testimony. The verse displays the model of humble service: the apostle sharing the labor, undeterred by his dignity. The verse sets up the miracle: Paul's preservation from the venom (v.5) and the natives' reaction (vv.4, 6). The verse displays the providence preparing a display of God's power: the viper's attack, far from harming Paul, would become the occasion for the islanders to behold the hand of God. Paul's humility and the sudden peril open the account of the miracle that would astonish Malta.
4:
"And when the barbarians saw the venomous beast hang on his hand, they said among themselves, No doubt this man is a murderer, whom, though he hath escaped the sea, yet vengeance suffereth not to live." The witnesses note the natives' superstitious judgment. On "no doubt this man is a murderer," the grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and others note the natives, seeing the viper fasten on Paul (a prisoner), concluded he must be a guilty murderer pursued by divine vengeance — punished in the hand, the instrument of murder.

On "vengeance" (Dike), the witnesses note the pagan notion. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note Dike was the goddess of Justice/Vengeance, daughter of Jupiter, who pursued the guilty. The natives reasoned from a true principle (that God punishes sin) but applied it falsely.

The Reformed Calvin gives the careful theology: the conviction that calamity signals God's wrath is implanted in all, even barbarians, and is not wholly false; but the error is to judge every sufferer guilty (as Job's friends did), forgetting that God afflicts the godly too, and that we must wait for the end. The verse displays the natives' superstition and the true principle they distorted.

The witnesses agree: the natives, seeing the viper hang on Paul (a prisoner), judged him a murderer pursued by divine Vengeance (Dike, the pagan goddess of Justice, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) — punished though he escaped the sea, struck in the hand, the instrument of murder. Calvin gives the careful theology: the conviction that calamity signals God's wrath is implanted in all (even barbarians) and is not wholly false; but the error is to judge every sufferer guilty (as Job's friends did), forgetting that God afflicts the godly too, and that we must suspend judgment and wait for the end. The verse displays the natives' superstition and the true principle they distorted. The verse marks the false inference: from calamity to guilt. The verse displays the implanted sense of divine justice (even in the heathen) and its corruption (judging every sufferer guilty). The verse displays the danger of reading every affliction as a particular judgment: the error of Job's friends. The natives' superstition, their notion of Vengeance, display both the universal sense of God's justice and the folly of judging by outward calamity. The verse displays the true principle (God punishes sin) wrongly applied to the innocent Paul.
5:
"And he shook off the beast into the fire, and felt no harm." The witnesses note Paul's deliverance from the viper — the fulfillment of Christ's promise. On "shook off," the grammatical voices note the calm action. Robertson notes the verb (apotinasso, to shake off). Paul, unafraid, shook the serpent into the fire.

On "felt no harm," the witnesses note the miracle. Barnes notes the remarkable fulfillment of Christ's promise: "They shall take up serpents... and it shall not hurt them" (Mark 16:18; Luke 10:19). The venom had no power over Paul.

The Reformed Calvin notes the token of a quiet mind: faith does not remove the feeling of danger, but tempers it, so Paul, trusting God's promise, was not troubled, ready to die if need be. The verse displays Paul's preservation and the fulfillment of Mark 16:18.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul calmly shook the viper into the fire (apotinasso, Robertson) and felt no harm — the remarkable fulfillment of Christ's promise (Mark 16:18; Luke 10:19), the venom powerless over him (Barnes). Calvin notes the token of a quiet mind: faith does not remove the feeling of danger but tempers it, so Paul, trusting God's promise, was untroubled, ready to die if need be. The verse displays Paul's preservation: shaking off the viper, unharmed. The verse marks the fulfillment of Christ's promise: the serpent's bite without effect. The verse displays the miracle: the deadly venom rendered powerless. The verse displays Paul's calm faith: shaking off the serpent without fear. The verse displays the power of God protecting his servant: the viper harmless. The preservation of Paul, the fulfillment of Mark 16:18, display God's faithful protection and the truth of Christ's word. The verse displays the apostle's quiet confidence: trusting the promise, undisturbed by the danger. The verse displays the sign that would astonish the natives (v.6): Paul unharmed by the deadly bite. The verse marks the miracle that vindicated Paul before the islanders and prepared the way for his ministry. Paul's preservation, the fulfillment of Christ's promise, display the power of God over the serpent and the protection of the apostle as he journeyed toward Rome to bear his witness. The verse displays both the miracle and Paul's calm faith resting on the divine promise.
6:
"Howbeit they looked when he should have swollen, or fallen down dead suddenly: but after they had looked a great while, and saw no harm come to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a god." The witnesses note the natives' fickle reversal. On "looked when he should have swollen," the grammatical voices note the expectation. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note the natives expected Paul to swell up (inflammation from the venom) or drop dead suddenly — the known effects of a viper's bite.

On "changed their minds... that he was a god," the witnesses note the fickleness. Seeing no harm, the natives swung to the opposite extreme: from murderer to god. Robertson notes the parallel with Lystra (14:11), where Paul was first taken for a god, then stoned — "so fickle is popular favour."

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the medium they missed: "He is neither a murderer nor a god, but a man of God." Calvin notes the human bent to superstition: robbing God of his glory by deifying creatures. The verse displays the natives' fickle reversal from murderer to god.

The witnesses agree: the natives, expecting Paul to swell up or drop dead from the venom (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson), saw no harm and swung to the opposite extreme — from murderer to god. Robertson notes the parallel with Lystra (14:11): so fickle is popular favour. Wesley notes the medium they missed: neither murderer nor god, but a man of God. Calvin notes the human bent to superstition: robbing God of his glory by deifying creatures. The verse displays the natives' fickle reversal. The verse marks the swing of popular opinion: from condemnation to deification. The verse displays the fickleness of the crowd: the same Paul a murderer one hour, a god the next. The verse displays the contrast with Lystra: there from god to stoned, here from murderer to god — the instability of popular judgment. The verse displays the human bent to superstition: deifying the creature, robbing God of his glory. The fickle reversal, from murderer to god, displays the folly of the natural mind in judging the children of God. The verse displays the truth Wesley names: Paul was neither murderer nor god, but a man of God. The verse displays the miracle's effect: astonishment swinging to misplaced worship. The verse displays the readiness of the heathen to fall into idolatry, deifying the man through whom God's power worked. The natives' fickle reversal displays both the miracle's impact and the corruption of the natural mind, ever extreme, ever prone to superstition. The verse sets up Paul's ministry on the island (vv.7-10).
7:
"In the same quarters were possessions of the chief man of the island, whose name was Publius; who received us, and lodged us three days courteously." The witnesses note the hospitality of Publius. On "the chief man of the island," the grammatical voices note the official title. Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson note "the first man" (protos) was an actual title of the Roman governor of Malta, attested by inscriptions — a mark of Luke's accuracy.

On Publius, the witnesses note the Roman name and rank. He was the principal man of the island, governor or chief landowner, who received and lodged the company (or a chosen few, including Paul) courteously for three days.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence and reward: Publius, perhaps moved by the miracle, honored Paul; and his hospitality would be richly repaid by the healing of his father (v.8) — God rewarding kindness to strangers. The verse displays the hospitality of Publius.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Publius, "the chief man of the island" (protos, an actual title of the Maltese governor, attested by inscriptions, a mark of Luke's accuracy, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson), received and lodged the company courteously for three days. Calvin notes the providence and reward: Publius, perhaps moved by the miracle, honored Paul, and his hospitality would be richly repaid by the healing of his father (v.8) — God rewarding kindness to strangers. The verse displays the hospitality of Publius: a generous reception. The verse marks the accuracy of Luke: the precise official title. The verse displays the providence: the chief man's kindness opening the way for Paul's ministry. The verse displays the courtesy of Publius: lodging the company for three days. The verse displays God's providence rewarding hospitality: the kindness to be repaid by a healing. The hospitality of Publius, his courteous reception, display the providence preparing for Paul's ministry and the reward of kindness to strangers. The verse displays the accuracy of the historian: the official title confirmed by inscriptions. The verse displays the welcome that opened Paul's ministry on Malta: the chief man's hospitality leading to the healing of his father and many others (vv.8-9). The verse marks the providence raising up a kind host whose generosity would be richly repaid. Publius's hospitality, his courteous reception, display both human kindness and the providence of God preparing the way for the gospel's witness through Paul's healings on the island.
8:
"And it came to pass, that the father of Publius lay sick of a fever and of a bloody flux: to whom Paul entered in, and prayed, and laid his hands on him, and healed him." The witnesses note the healing of Publius's father. On the disease, the grammatical voices note Luke's medical precision. Robertson, Ellicott, and Clarke note "fevers and dysentery" — medical terms (the plural "fevers" indicating recurrent attacks), a critical case (Hippocrates).

On "prayed, and laid his hands on him," the witnesses note the manner. Calvin and Clarke note Paul prayed (declaring himself the minister, not the author, of the miracle) and laid on hands — the union reminding of James 5:14-15, and the juxtaposition with the serpent of Mark 16:18.

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul, by prayer, gave God the glory: he humbly craved the man's life of God, professing himself only the minister. The verse displays the healing — the gift of healing exercised through prayer and laying on of hands.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Publius's father, sick of fevers and dysentery (Luke's medical precision, the plural indicating recurrent attacks, a critical case, Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke), was healed by Paul, who prayed and laid hands on him — the union reminding of James 5:14-15 and the juxtaposition with Mark 16:18. Calvin notes Paul, by prayer, gave God the glory: humbly craving the man's life of God, professing himself only the minister, not the author. The verse displays the healing: the gift exercised through prayer and laying on of hands. The verse marks Luke's medical precision: the named diseases. The verse displays Paul the minister, not the author: giving God the glory by prayer. The verse displays the manner of the miracle: prayer and the laying on of hands, as James directs. The verse displays the gift of healing: the critical illness cured. The healing of Publius's father, through prayer and laying on of hands, displays both the power of God and the humility of his minister. The verse displays the reward of Publius's hospitality: his father healed. The verse displays the apostle's method: prayer first, giving God the glory, then the laying on of hands. The verse displays the providence: the miracle opening the way for the healing of many (v.9) and the honoring of Paul (v.10). The healing of Publius's father, through Paul's prayer and laying on of hands, displays the power of God working through his humble minister and confirming the gospel on Malta.
9:
"So when this was done, others also, which had diseases in the island, came, and were healed:" The brief verse notes the wider healings on Malta. The grammatical voices note the rush of the sick. Robertson and Ellicott note the article ("the others who had infirmities") and the imperfect tense — a continuing stream of the island's sick coming and being healed.

On the distinction, the witnesses note Luke's terms. Robertson notes Ramsay's view that iaomai (Paul's miraculous healing, v.8) is distinguished from therapeuo (the cures, v.9), perhaps including Luke the physician's share — though the distinction is general.

On Luke the physician, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes Luke, a physician, is not even consulted: Paul enters, prays, lays on hands, and heals — the miraculous power, not medical art. The verse displays the wider ministry of healing on the island.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: after the healing of Publius's father, a continuing stream of the island's sick came and were healed (the imperfect tense, the article, Robertson, Ellicott) — a wider ministry of healing on Malta. Clarke notes Luke the physician was not even consulted: Paul entered, prayed, laid on hands, and healed — miraculous power, not medical art. Robertson notes the possible distinction (iaomai of Paul's miracles, therapeuo of the cures). The verse displays the wider ministry of healing: the sick of the island coming to Paul. The verse marks the spread of the miracle: from Publius's father to many. The verse displays the power of God confirming the gospel: the island's sick healed. The verse displays the rush of the sick: a continuing stream coming to be healed. The verse displays the contrast: Luke the physician not consulted, the miraculous power of God at work. The wider healings on Malta display the power of God through his apostle and the confirmation of the gospel on the island. The verse displays the providence: the three-month sojourn (v.11) marked by a ministry of healing. The verse displays the apostle's compassion and power: healing all who came. The verse displays the gospel's confirmation among the heathen: the miracles winning honor and opening hearts. The wider healings, the stream of the sick, display the power of God displayed through Paul during the Malta sojourn, confirming his ministry and preparing for the honors that would speed the company on their way (v.10). The verse marks the abundant grace of healing poured out on the island.
10:
"Who also honoured us with many honours; and when we departed, they laded us with such things as were necessary." The witnesses note the gratitude of the Maltese. On "honoured us with many honours," the grammatical voices note the term. Robertson notes the word (timais) was often used for payment for professional services (an honorarium) — the islanders honoring the company, perhaps with gifts, in gratitude for the healings.

On "laded us with such things as were necessary," the witnesses note the provision. At their departure (after three months, v.11), the grateful islanders supplied the company with all needful things for the voyage.

The witnesses note the providence and the reward: the kindness shown to Paul repaid, and the company furnished for the rest of the journey. Lightfoot and others note the abundant supply. The verse displays the gratitude and generosity of the Maltese.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the grateful Maltese honored the company with many honors (timais, often used for an honorarium, Robertson) and, at their departure, supplied them with all needful things for the voyage. The verse displays the gratitude and generosity of the islanders: honoring and provisioning the company. The verse marks the reward of Paul's ministry: the healings repaid with honor and provision. The verse displays the providence: the company furnished for the rest of the journey. The verse displays the islanders' thankfulness: honoring Paul and his companions for the healings. The verse displays the abundant supply: laded with all that was necessary. The gratitude and generosity of the Maltese display both human thankfulness and the providence of God furnishing his servant for the journey. The verse displays the fruit of Paul's ministry: the island grateful, the company honored and provisioned. The verse displays the providence caring for Paul: the kindness shown repaid, the needs supplied. The verse marks the close of the Malta sojourn: the company honored and furnished, ready to resume the voyage to Rome (v.11). The gratitude of the Maltese, their honors and provisions, display the reward of the gospel's ministry and the providence of God supplying every need as Paul journeyed toward the imperial city. The verse displays the abundant kindness that crowned the three months on Malta.
11:
"And after three months we departed in a ship of Alexandria, which had wintered in the isle, whose sign was Castor and Pollux." The witnesses note the resumption of the voyage. On "after three months," the grammatical voices note the time. The company wintered on Malta (navigation suspended in the stormy season) and departed when sailing resumed in spring.

On the ship, the witnesses note the Alexandrian grain ship that had wintered at Malta. On "Castor and Pollux," Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the figurehead — the Twin Brothers (Dioscuri, sons of Zeus), regarded by sailors as patrons and protectors of seafarers.

The puritan Henry and others note the providence and the irony: the ship bore the sign of the pagan twin gods, yet it was the true God who protected Paul. The verse displays the resumption of the voyage toward Rome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after three months wintering on Malta (navigation suspended in the stormy season), the company departed in an Alexandrian grain ship that had wintered there, bearing the figurehead of Castor and Pollux (the Twin Brothers, the Dioscuri, sons of Zeus, patrons of sailors, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Henry notes the irony: the ship bore the sign of the pagan twin gods, yet the true God protected Paul. The verse displays the resumption of the voyage toward Rome. The verse marks the transition from Malta to the final leg of the journey. The verse displays the providence: the company resuming the voyage in spring. The verse displays the detail of the ship: an Alexandrian grain ship with its pagan figurehead. The verse displays the irony: the sign of the twin gods, yet the true God the protector. The resumption of the voyage, the ship of Castor and Pollux, display the providence carrying Paul onward to Rome. The verse displays the accuracy of Luke: the precise detail of the ship and its figurehead. The verse displays the providence in the timing: the winter past, the voyage resumed. The verse marks the final stage of the journey: from Malta toward Italy and Rome. The resumption of the voyage, the Alexandrian ship, display the providence of God carrying his servant toward the imperial city where he would bear his witness. The verse sets up the stops at Syracuse, Rhegium, and Puteoli (vv.12-13) and the arrival in Italy. The verse displays the steady advance of Paul's journey under the protecting hand of God.
12:
"And landing at Syracuse, we tarried there three days." The brief verse notes the first stop on the voyage from Malta. The grammatical voices note Syracuse, the chief city of Sicily, a notable seaport about 80 miles from Malta. The company landed and stayed three days, perhaps for trade or favorable winds.

On the stop, the witnesses note the natural route: the Alexandrian ship calling at Syracuse on its way to Italy. The three days may have been for unloading, loading, or waiting for wind.

The witnesses note the providence in the steady progress: the company advancing stage by stage toward Rome. The verse displays the first leg of the resumed voyage.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the company landed at Syracuse (the chief city of Sicily, a notable seaport about 80 miles from Malta) and tarried three days — perhaps for trade or favorable winds, the natural route of the Alexandrian ship toward Italy. The verse displays the first leg of the resumed voyage: the stop at Syracuse. The verse marks the steady progress: the company advancing stage by stage. The verse displays the providence: the journey continuing toward Rome. The verse displays the detail of the route: Syracuse, the Sicilian seaport. The verse displays the natural course of the voyage: the grain ship calling at the major ports. The stop at Syracuse, the three days' stay, display the steady advance of Paul's journey under God's providence. The verse displays the accuracy of Luke: the named port and the duration. The verse displays the providence in the ordinary course of travel: the ship calling at Syracuse, the company drawing nearer to Rome. The verse marks one stage of the final leg: from Malta through Syracuse toward Rhegium and Puteoli (v.13). The stop at Syracuse displays the providence carrying Paul onward, stage by stage, toward the imperial city where his witness awaited. The verse, brief as it is, marks the steady, providential progress of the apostle's journey to Rome, the goal toward which the whole narrative has been moving. The verse displays God's faithful guidance of his servant along the path he had appointed (23:11; 27:24).
13:
"And from thence we fetched a compass, and came to Rhegium: and after one day the south wind blew, and we came the next day to Puteoli:" The witnesses note the voyage's progress to Puteoli. On "fetched a compass," the grammatical voices note the manoeuvre. Robertson and others note the ship tacked or circled (made a circuit) to reach Rhegium (modern Reggio), on the toe of Italy, opposite Sicily.

On "the south wind" and Puteoli, the witnesses note the favorable wind that carried them swiftly. Puteoli (modern Pozzuoli), near Naples, was the chief port of southern Italy, where the Alexandrian grain ships landed. The company reached it the next day.

The witnesses note the providence in the favorable wind and the arrival in Italy. Puteoli marked the effective end of the sea voyage; from there Paul would travel by land to Rome. The verse displays the arrival in Italy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ship made a circuit (tacked, Robertson) to Rhegium (on the toe of Italy, opposite Sicily), then, with a favorable south wind, reached Puteoli (Pozzuoli, near Naples, the chief port of southern Italy where the Alexandrian grain ships landed) the next day. The verse displays the arrival in Italy: the effective end of the sea voyage. The verse marks the providence: the favorable wind carrying the company swiftly. The verse displays the progress to Puteoli: Rhegium, then the south wind, then the chief Italian port. The verse displays the detail of the route: the manoeuvre, the wind, the ports. The verse displays the providence in the favorable wind: the swift arrival in Italy. The arrival at Puteoli, the favorable south wind, display the providence carrying Paul to Italy, near the end of his long journey. The verse displays the accuracy of Luke: the named ports and the wind. The verse displays the providence speeding the company: the south wind a token of God's favor. The verse marks the arrival on Italian soil: from Puteoli, Paul would travel by land to Rome (v.16). The arrival in Italy, the favorable voyage to Puteoli, display the providence bringing Paul to the threshold of Rome, the goal of his journey. The verse displays the steady advance toward the imperial city, where the apostle would bear his witness before all. The verse sets up the meeting with the brethren (vv.14-15) and the entry into Rome.
14:
"Where we found brethren, and were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we went toward Rome." The witnesses note the brethren at Puteoli. On "found brethren," the grammatical voices note the established church. There were already Christians at Puteoli — the gospel had spread to this Italian port. They invited Paul and his company to stay seven days.

On the seven days, the witnesses note the providence and the permission. Robertson and others note the centurion Julius (favorably disposed to Paul) allowed the week's stay, perhaps for the ship's business or the soldiers' rest. The seven days allowed fellowship with the brethren.

On "we went toward Rome," the witnesses note the final stage by land. From Puteoli, the company traveled overland to Rome. The verse displays the fellowship at Puteoli and the approach to Rome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: at Puteoli the company found brethren (an established church, the gospel already spread to this Italian port) who invited them to stay seven days — the centurion Julius (favorably disposed) allowing the week's stay (Robertson), giving Paul fellowship with the brethren before the final overland stage to Rome. The verse displays the fellowship at Puteoli: the brethren receiving Paul. The verse marks the spread of the gospel: Christians already at Puteoli. The verse displays the providence: the week of fellowship granted. The verse displays the kindness of Julius: allowing the stay. The verse displays the approach to Rome: the final stage by land. The fellowship at Puteoli, the seven days with the brethren, display the comfort of Christian fellowship and the spread of the gospel through Italy. The verse displays the providence: Paul, a prisoner, yet refreshed by the brethren. The verse displays the favor shown Paul: the centurion permitting the week's stay. The verse marks the last stage of the journey: from Puteoli toward Rome. The fellowship at Puteoli displays both the spread of the gospel and the providence comforting Paul with the brethren before his entry into the imperial city. The verse sets up the meeting with the Roman brethren at Appii Forum and Three Taverns (v.15) and the arrival at Rome (v.16). The verse displays the encouragement of Christian fellowship as Paul drew near to Rome.
15:
"And from thence, when the brethren heard of us, they came to meet us as far as Appii forum, and The three taverns: whom when Paul saw, he thanked God, and took courage." The witnesses note the Roman brethren coming to meet Paul. On the meeting, the grammatical voices note the distances. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note the Roman Christians, hearing of Paul's arrival, came out to meet him — some as far as Appii Forum (about 40 miles from Rome), some to the Three Taverns (about 30 miles) — on the Appian Way.

On "thanked God, and took courage," the witnesses note Paul's encouragement. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson note Paul, who had long desired to see the Roman church (Romans 1:9-11), was strengthened by their love — thanking God and taking fresh courage. Robertson notes Christ had bidden him be of good courage (23:11).

The Reformed Calvin notes the value of fellowship: God comforted Paul through the brethren, that he might more joyfully defend the gospel; the godly are strengthened by means of one another. The verse displays the brethren's love and Paul's encouragement.

The witnesses agree: the Roman Christians, hearing of Paul's arrival, came out to meet him on the Appian Way — some to Appii Forum (40 miles from Rome), some to the Three Taverns (30 miles, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson); and Paul, long desiring to see the Roman church (Romans 1:9-11), thanked God and took fresh courage at their love. Calvin notes the value of fellowship: God comforted Paul through the brethren, that he might more joyfully defend the gospel; the godly are strengthened by one another. The verse displays the brethren's love and Paul's encouragement. The verse marks the welcome of the Roman church: coming far to meet the prisoner-apostle. The verse displays the comfort of fellowship: Paul strengthened by the brethren. The verse displays the courage renewed: the apostle heartened for the trials ahead. The verse displays the love of the brethren: traveling far to honor and encourage Paul. The brethren's love, Paul's thanksgiving and courage, display the strengthening power of Christian fellowship. The verse displays the providence: God comforting Paul through the brethren before his entry into Rome. The verse displays the answer to Paul's long desire (Romans 1): meeting the Roman church at last, though in bonds. The verse marks the encouragement that prepared Paul for Rome: the brethren's love renewing his courage. The Roman brethren's welcome, Paul's thanksgiving and renewed courage, display the comfort of fellowship and the providence of God strengthening his servant for the witness ahead in the imperial city.
16:
"And when we came to Rome, the centurion delivered the prisoners to the captain of the guard: but Paul was suffered to dwell by himself with a soldier that kept him." The witnesses note Paul's arrival at Rome and his relative liberty. On "the captain of the guard," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the prisoners were delivered to the commander of the Praetorian Guard (perhaps Burrus, a note of time) — the custodian of prisoners sent from the provinces.

On "Paul was suffered to dwell by himself," the witnesses note the favor. Robertson and Ellicott note Paul was allowed a custodia libera — dwelling in his own lodging, chained to a single soldier, not in the common prison. The favor was likely due to Julius's good report and Festus's testimony of his innocence.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: God governed Paul's bonds, granting him liberty not merely to ease his trouble but that the faithful might have free access to him, and the treasure of the gospel not be shut up in prison. The verse displays the arrival at Rome and the providential liberty.

The witnesses agree: at Rome the prisoners were delivered to the captain of the Praetorian Guard (perhaps Burrus, a note of time, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), but Paul was allowed to dwell by himself (a custodia libera, in his own lodging, chained to a single soldier, Robertson, Ellicott) — the favor due to Julius's good report and Festus's testimony of his innocence. Calvin notes the providence: God governed Paul's bonds, granting him liberty not merely to ease his trouble but that the faithful might have free access and the gospel not be shut up in prison. The verse displays the arrival at Rome and the providential liberty. The verse marks the goal reached: Paul at Rome, as Christ had promised (23:11). The verse displays the favor shown Paul: dwelling by himself, not in the common prison. The verse displays the providence in the liberty: the gospel free to spread. The verse displays the chain that remained: Paul still a prisoner, bound to a soldier. The arrival at Rome, the providential liberty, display God's faithfulness in bringing Paul to the imperial city and granting him freedom to minister. The verse displays the providence ordering even the conditions of Paul's custody for the gospel's advance. The verse marks the fulfillment of the long-appointed journey: Paul at Rome, free to receive all who came (v.30) and to preach the kingdom (v.31). The arrival at Rome, the relative liberty, display the providence of God bringing his servant to the goal and opening the door for his unhindered witness. The verse displays Christ's promise kept: Paul bearing witness at Rome.
17:
"And it came to pass, that after three days Paul called the chief of the Jews together: and when they were come together, he said unto them, Men and brethren, though I have committed nothing against the people, or customs of our fathers, yet was I delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans." The witnesses note Paul's first act at Rome: calling the chief Jews. On the promptness, the grammatical voices note Paul, ever beginning with his own nation, called the Jewish leaders within three days — his unwearied love for Israel.

On his defense, the witnesses note the substance. Paul declared his innocence: he had committed nothing against the Jewish people or the customs of the fathers, yet was delivered a prisoner to the Romans. He explained his case without bitterness, not accusing his nation.

Chrysostom and the puritan Henry note Paul's meekness and wisdom: conciliating the Jews, addressing them as brethren, stating his case mildly to gain a hearing for the gospel. The verse displays Paul's prompt outreach to the Jews and his temperate defense.

The witnesses agree: Paul, within three days of arriving at Rome, called the chief Jews together (his unwearied love for Israel, ever beginning with his own nation) and declared his innocence — that he had committed nothing against the Jewish people or the customs of the fathers, yet was delivered a prisoner to the Romans, stating his case without bitterness. Chrysostom and Henry note his meekness and wisdom: conciliating the Jews, addressing them as brethren, to gain a hearing for the gospel. The verse displays Paul's prompt outreach and temperate defense. The verse marks his consistent method: beginning with the Jews. The verse displays his love for his nation: calling the leaders at once. The verse displays his innocence: nothing committed against the people or the law. The verse displays his temperate spirit: explaining without accusing. The prompt outreach, the mild defense, display Paul's love for Israel and his wisdom in seeking a hearing. The verse displays the apostle's heart: even a prisoner, beginning with his own people. The verse displays the providence: the gospel offered first to the Jews at Rome. The verse marks the opening of Paul's Roman ministry: addressing the Jewish leaders, stating his case, preparing to expound the gospel (v.23). Paul's prompt outreach to the Jews, his temperate defense, display his unwearied love for Israel and his wisdom in conciliating his hearers to win them for Christ. The verse displays the apostle, even in bonds, faithful to his calling: to the Jew first, then the Gentile.
18:
"Who, when they had examined me, would have let me go, because there was no cause of death in me." The brief verse notes the Roman verdict of innocence. The grammatical voices note Paul's appeal to the Roman examination: the authorities (Felix, Festus, and especially the hearing before Agrippa, 26:31-32) had found no capital charge against him and would have released him.

On "no cause of death," the witnesses note the repeated acquittals. Barnes and Robertson note Paul had been examined and found guiltless by the Roman officials — there was no ground for a death sentence.

The witnesses note Paul's purpose: to show the Jews at Rome that his imprisonment was not for any crime, and that even the Romans acknowledged his innocence. The verse displays the Roman testimony to Paul's innocence.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Paul appealed to the Roman examination — the authorities (Felix, Festus, and the hearing before Agrippa, 26:31-32) had found no capital charge against him and would have released him, there being no cause of death (Barnes, Robertson). The verse displays the Roman testimony to Paul's innocence: the repeated acquittals. The verse marks Paul's purpose: showing the Jews that his bonds were not for any crime. The verse displays the Roman verdict: no ground for a death sentence. The verse displays the vindication of Paul: even the Romans acknowledged his innocence. The Roman testimony, the verdict of innocence, display the injustice of Paul's imprisonment and the vindication of the apostle. The verse displays the contrast: the Romans willing to release him, the Jews pursuing him. The verse displays the providence: Paul's innocence attested by the very authorities. The verse marks the ground of Paul's appeal to Caesar (v.19): not guilt, but the Jews' opposition forcing his hand. The Roman testimony to Paul's innocence displays both the injustice he suffered and the vindication of his cause, that the Jews at Rome might know he was no criminal but a faithful servant of God, bound for the hope of Israel (v.20). The verse displays the apostle establishing his innocence before his nation, that the gospel might have a fair hearing. The verse sets up the explanation of his appeal to Caesar (v.19).
19:
"But when the Jews spake against it, I was constrained to appeal unto Caesar; not that I had ought to accuse my nation of." The witnesses note Paul's explanation of his appeal to Caesar. On "the Jews spake against it," the grammatical voices note the constraint. Barnes and Robertson note that when the Jews opposed his release (seeking his death), Paul was forced to appeal to Caesar to save his life (25:11).

On "not that I had ought to accuse my nation," the witnesses note Paul's tender care. He hastens to add that he appealed not to accuse the Jews, but only in self-defense — his love for his nation undimmed, no charge brought against them.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Calvin note Paul's meekness: even in appealing to Caesar, he bore no grudge against his people, seeking not their harm but his own safety. The verse displays Paul's appeal and his tender care for his nation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when the Jews opposed his release (seeking his death), Paul was constrained to appeal to Caesar to save his life (25:11, Barnes, Robertson) — yet not to accuse his nation, his love for Israel undimmed, no charge brought against them. Wesley and Calvin note Paul's meekness: even in appealing to Caesar, he bore no grudge, seeking not their harm but his own safety. The verse displays Paul's appeal and his tender care for his nation. The verse marks the constraint: the Jews' opposition forcing the appeal. The verse displays Paul's love for his people: appealing not to accuse them. The verse displays the apostle's meekness: no bitterness, no charge against Israel. The verse displays the reason for the appeal: self-defense, not vengeance. Paul's appeal and his tender care display his love for his nation even amid their hostility. The verse displays the contrast: the Jews seeking his death, Paul refusing to accuse them. The verse displays the providence: the appeal to Caesar (forced by the Jews) bringing Paul to Rome, as God had purposed (23:11). The verse marks Paul's wisdom and love: explaining the appeal without reproach. Paul's appeal to Caesar, his refusal to accuse his nation, display both the constraint of the Jews' hostility and the apostle's unwearied love for Israel. The verse displays the meekness of Paul: bearing no grudge, seeking his people's good even as he defended his life. The verse sets up his declaration of the true ground of his bonds: the hope of Israel (v.20).
20:
"For this cause therefore have I called for you, to see you, and to speak with you: because that for the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain." The witnesses note the true ground of Paul's bonds: the hope of Israel. On "the hope of Israel," the grammatical voices note its meaning. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson note the two-fold hope: the expectation of the Messiah and the hope of the resurrection of the dead — both grounded in Christ (Romans 1:3-4), the resurrection of Jesus proving him the Messiah.

On "bound with this chain," the witnesses note the pathos. Ellicott notes the singular "chain" agrees with the custodia libera (v.16) — Paul chained to a single soldier. There is a touch of pathos in his appeal to his bonds (cf. Ephesians 6:20).

The Reformed Calvin notes the heart of it: Paul is bound for the hope of Israel because the whole hope of the godly rests in Christ — no man hopes aright but he who looks to Christ and his spiritual kingdom. The verse displays the true ground of Paul's bonds: the Messianic and resurrection hope.

The witnesses agree: Paul declared the true ground of his bonds — "the hope of Israel," the two-fold hope of the Messiah and of the resurrection of the dead (both grounded in Christ, the resurrection of Jesus proving him the Messiah, Romans 1:3-4, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson) — bound with a single chain (the custodia libera, Ellicott), a touch of pathos in his appeal. Calvin notes the heart of it: Paul is bound for the hope of Israel because the whole hope of the godly rests in Christ — no man hopes aright but he who looks to Christ and his spiritual kingdom. The verse displays the true ground of Paul's bonds. The verse marks the heart of his defense: not crime, but the hope of Israel. The verse displays the Messianic and resurrection hope: the very hope Israel professed, for which Paul suffered. The verse displays the irony: Paul bound for the very hope his accusers claimed to cherish. The verse displays the pathos: the apostle in chains for the gospel. The true ground of Paul's bonds, the hope of Israel, displays the continuity of the gospel with Israel's hope and the injustice of his suffering. The verse displays the centrality of the resurrection: the hope for which Paul was bound. The verse displays Christ as the true hope of Israel: the Messiah, the risen Lord, in whom all godly hope rests (Calvin). The verse marks Paul's appeal to common ground: the hope Israel shared, fulfilled in Christ. The hope of Israel, the ground of Paul's bonds, displays the gospel as the fulfillment of Israel's hope and the apostle's suffering for the risen Christ, the true hope of his nation and of all who believe.
21:
"And they said unto him, We neither received letters out of Judaea concerning thee, neither any of the brethren that came shewed or spake any harm of thee." The witnesses note the Jews' reply: no charge had reached them. On the letters, the grammatical voices note the absence of accusation. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the Jerusalem Jews had sent no official letters against Paul, and no traveler had spoken evil of him — perhaps because they saw their cause as hopeless (the winter suspending sea travel, or their abandoning the prosecution).

On the silence, the witnesses note the providence. Clarke notes the Jews knew their cause was hopeless and did not press it; Wesley notes "there must have been a peculiar providence in this," the bridle in their enemies' mouths.

The Reformed Calvin notes their contempt or despair: not gentleness toward Paul, but because they could not oppress him so far off, and his removal to Rome seemed to them as good as a grave. The verse displays the Jews' reply and the providence in the silence.

The witnesses agree: the Roman Jews replied that no letters from Judaea and no traveler had brought any charge or evil report against Paul — perhaps because the Jerusalem Jews saw their cause as hopeless (the winter suspending travel, or their abandoning the prosecution, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Wesley notes the peculiar providence: the bridle in the enemies' mouths. Calvin notes their contempt or despair: not gentleness, but because they could not oppress Paul so far off, his removal to Rome seeming to them a grave. The verse displays the Jews' reply and the providence in the silence. The verse marks the absence of accusation: no charge reaching Rome. The verse displays the providence: God restraining the enemies, their cause unpressed. The verse displays the silence of the accusers: no evil report against Paul. The verse displays the hopelessness of the Jews' case: abandoned, not pursued to Rome. The Jews' reply, the providence in the silence, display God's hand restraining Paul's enemies and clearing the way for his witness. The verse displays the favorable opening: no prejudice against Paul among the Roman Jews. The verse displays the providence: the enemies silenced, the door open for the gospel. The verse marks the readiness of the Roman Jews to hear Paul (v.22): no charge against him, willing to learn his views. The Jews' reply, the absence of accusation, display the providence preparing a fair hearing for the gospel at Rome. The verse displays God's hand restraining the malice of Paul's enemies and opening the way for his unhindered witness.
22:
"But we desire to hear of thee what thou thinkest: for as concerning this sect, we know that every where it is spoken against." The witnesses note the Jews' request to hear Paul's views. On "we desire to hear what thou thinkest," the grammatical voices note the openness. Robertson notes they think it fair to hear Paul's side; they wish an authoritative exposition of his views, identifying him with "this sect."

On "this sect... every where spoken against," the witnesses note the reputation of Christianity. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the Christians were everywhere maligned (as Tacitus and Suetonius attest) — a "sect" detested and slandered, the calumnies spread by the Jews and the heathen alike.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the irony: "This is no proof at all of a bad cause, but a very probable mark of a good one." The world's hostility is no condemnation of the truth. The verse displays the Jews' request and the maligned reputation of the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Roman Jews requested to hear Paul's views, thinking it fair to hear his side (Robertson), identifying him with "this sect" — Christianity, everywhere spoken against (maligned and slandered, as Tacitus and Suetonius attest, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott). Wesley notes the irony: the world's hostility is no proof of a bad cause, but a probable mark of a good one. The verse displays the Jews' request and the maligned reputation of the gospel. The verse marks the openness of the Roman Jews: willing to hear Paul out. The verse displays the reputation of Christianity: everywhere spoken against. The verse displays the slander against the faith: the "sect" detested by Jew and heathen. The verse displays the irony Wesley names: opposition no condemnation of the truth. The Jews' request, the maligned reputation, display both the fair hearing granted Paul and the world's hostility to the gospel. The verse displays the opportunity: Paul invited to expound his views (v.23). The verse displays the providence: the door opened for the gospel at Rome. The verse marks the setting for the great exposition (v.23): the Jews willing to hear, Paul ready to declare the kingdom. The Jews' request, the maligned reputation of the gospel, display the fair hearing providence granted Paul and the truth that the world's hostility is no mark against the faith. The verse displays the opening for Paul's full exposition of the gospel to the Jews at Rome, the climax of his apostolic witness.
23:
"And when they had appointed him a day, there came many to him into his lodging; to whom he expounded and testified the kingdom of God, persuading them concerning Jesus, both out of the law of Moses, and out of the prophets, from morning till evening." The witnesses note Paul's great day of exposition. On the method, the grammatical voices note the two-fold work. Robertson and Calvin note Paul expounded and testified the kingdom of God and persuaded concerning Jesus — from the Law and the Prophets, the Scriptures the Jews accepted, from morning till evening, a full day's labor.

On "the kingdom of God," the witnesses note its spiritual nature. Calvin notes Paul defined the kingdom rightly (against carnal Jewish notions): spiritual, beginning in newness of life, ending in heavenly glory; and persuaded them to receive Christ, the suffering Redeemer foretold by the prophets.

The puritan Henry notes Paul's diligence and the Jews' willingness to hear (more than at Jerusalem); the apostle laboring a full day to win them. The verse displays Paul's full exposition of the kingdom and persuasion concerning Jesus from the Scriptures.

The witnesses agree: on the appointed day, many came to Paul's lodging, and he expounded and testified the kingdom of God and persuaded concerning Jesus — from the Law and the Prophets, the Scriptures the Jews accepted, from morning till evening (Robertson, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul defined the kingdom rightly (against carnal Jewish notions): spiritual, beginning in newness of life, ending in heavenly glory; and urged them to receive Christ, the suffering Redeemer foretold by the prophets. Henry notes Paul's diligence and the Jews' willingness (more than at Jerusalem). The verse displays Paul's full exposition and persuasion from the Scriptures. The verse marks the great day: a full day's reasoning from the Scriptures. The verse displays the substance: the kingdom of God and Jesus the Christ. The verse displays the method: proving the gospel from the Law and the Prophets. The verse displays the spiritual kingdom: rightly defined against carnal expectations. Paul's full exposition, his persuasion from the Scriptures, display the apostle's scriptural method and tireless labor for his nation. The verse displays the unity of the Testaments: the gospel proved from Moses and the prophets. The verse displays the centrality of Christ: persuading them concerning Jesus. The verse marks the climax of Paul's witness to the Jews: a full day expounding the kingdom and the Christ. Paul's full exposition of the kingdom of God, his persuasion concerning Jesus from the Scriptures, display the apostle's scriptural method, his tireless labor, and the heart of the gospel: the kingdom of God and the suffering, risen Christ foretold by the prophets. The verse sets up the divided response (v.24) and the solemn citation of Isaiah (vv.25-27).
24:
"And some believed the things which were spoken, and some believed not." The brief verse notes the divided response. The grammatical voices note the imperfect tense. Robertson notes "some began to be persuaded" (inchoative) and "some continued to disbelieve" — the usual divided result of gospel preaching.

On the division, the witnesses note the perennial effect. Clarke notes his message was treated as the gospel still is: some believe and are converted, others remain in unbelief. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the stumbling-block: the cross, the spiritual nature of the kingdom, which many could not receive.

The Reformed Calvin notes the sovereignty: even Paul's grace-filled preaching could not win all; the unbelief of many resists the plainest doctrine, for faith is not common to all — those return blind who came willingly. The verse displays the divided response to the gospel.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: some believed, some believed not — "some began to be persuaded, some continued to disbelieve" (the imperfect tense, Robertson), the usual divided result of gospel preaching. Clarke notes the message treated as the gospel still is: some converted, others in unbelief, the cross and the spiritual kingdom the stumbling-block. Calvin notes the sovereignty: even Paul's grace-filled preaching could not win all, for faith is not common to all — those return blind who came willingly. The verse displays the divided response. The verse marks the perennial effect: the gospel dividing its hearers. The verse displays the two outcomes: belief and unbelief. The verse displays the stumbling-block: the cross, the spiritual kingdom. The verse displays the sovereignty of grace: faith not common to all. The divided response displays the perennial effect of the gospel: dividing belief from unbelief. The verse displays the mystery: the same word received by some, rejected by others. The verse displays the apostle's experience: even his preaching not winning all. The verse marks the occasion for the solemn citation of Isaiah (vv.25-27): the unbelief of many fulfilling the ancient prophecy. The divided response, belief and unbelief, displays both the dividing power of the gospel and the sovereignty of grace, and sets up Paul's solemn warning from Isaiah to the unbelieving. The verse displays the truth that the gospel, faithfully preached, ever divides its hearers — some believing unto life, some hardening unto judgment. The verse marks the sober reality of the gospel's reception among the Jews at Rome.
25:
"And when they agreed not among themselves, they departed, after that Paul had spoken one word, Well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias the prophet unto our fathers," The witnesses note Paul's solemn closing word from Isaiah. On "agreed not... departed," the grammatical voices note the discord. Robertson and Calvin note the Jews, divided, loosed themselves from Paul; and as they left, he spoke one last solemn word — the prophecy of Isaiah.

On "well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias," the witnesses note the inspiration. Barnes and Robertson note Paul ascribes the prophecy to the Holy Ghost speaking through Isaiah — a strong testimony to inspiration, and (Robertson) showing the Jews they resist God himself (cf. 7:51).

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: Paul, seeing their obstinacy, inveighs sharply, citing the Holy Ghost to give the oracle authority; the stubborn must be cited to God's judgment-seat. By naming the Spirit, Paul shows the word proceeds from God, not man. The verse displays Paul's solemn citation of Isaiah, ascribed to the Holy Ghost.

The witnesses agree: the Jews, divided and discordant, departed; and Paul spoke one last solemn word — "Well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias" — ascribing the prophecy to the Holy Ghost speaking through Isaiah (a strong testimony to inspiration, Barnes, Robertson), showing the Jews they resist God himself (cf. 7:51). Calvin notes the force: Paul, seeing their obstinacy, inveighs sharply, citing the Holy Ghost to give the oracle authority — the stubborn must be cited to God's judgment-seat; by naming the Spirit, Paul shows the word proceeds from God. The verse displays Paul's solemn citation, ascribed to the Holy Ghost. The verse marks the closing word: a solemn warning from Isaiah. The verse displays the inspiration of Scripture: the Holy Ghost speaking through the prophet. The verse displays the gravity: the unbelieving Jews resisting God himself. The verse displays Paul's sharp rebuke: the obstinate cited to God's judgment. Paul's solemn citation, ascribed to the Holy Ghost, displays both the inspiration of Scripture and the solemn warning to the unbelieving. The verse displays the authority of the oracle: proceeding from God, not man. The verse displays the apostle's final appeal: the ancient prophecy fulfilled in their unbelief. The verse sets up the quotation of Isaiah 6:9-10 (vv.26-27) and the turning to the Gentiles (v.28). Paul's solemn closing word, the Holy Ghost's testimony through Isaiah, displays the inspiration of Scripture and the gravity of the Jews' unbelief, resisting the very Spirit of God who spoke of old to their fathers.
26:
"Saying, Go unto this people, and say, Hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and not perceive:" The witnesses note the prophecy of judicial blindness (Isaiah 6:9). On the quotation, the grammatical voices note its frequency. Robertson and Calvin note this passage (Isaiah 6:9-10) is cited six times in the New Testament (Matthew 13, Mark 4, Luke 8, John 12, Romans 11, here) — applied by Christ to his use of parables and by John and Paul to the Jews' rejection of the Messiah.

On "hearing... shall not understand," the witnesses note the judicial blindness. The Jews would hear and see, yet not understand or perceive — a wilful blindness consequent on their resistance of the truth.

The Reformed Calvin gives the careful theology: the prophet attributes the blindness to God's secret judgment, yet the people shut their own eyes; both are true — God blinds the reprobate, yet they become blind of their own accord; the word's clarity leaves them inexcusable. The verse displays the prophecy of judicial blindness.

The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Isaiah 6:9 — "hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand" — the prophecy of judicial blindness, cited six times in the New Testament (Robertson, Calvin), applied by Christ to his parables and by John and Paul to the Jews' rejection of the Messiah. Calvin gives the careful theology: the prophet attributes the blindness to God's secret judgment, yet the people shut their own eyes; both are true — God blinds the reprobate, yet they become blind of their own accord, the word's clarity leaving them inexcusable. The verse displays the prophecy of judicial blindness. The verse marks the solemn warning: the Jews hearing yet not understanding. The verse displays the wilful blindness: consequent on resisting the truth. The verse displays the dual truth (Calvin): God's judgment and man's self-blinding. The verse displays the inexcusableness: the word clear, the blindness their own. The prophecy of judicial blindness displays both the sovereignty of God's judgment and the responsibility of the unbelieving. The verse displays the fulfillment: the ancient prophecy realized in the Jews' rejection. The verse displays the gravity: hearing the gospel yet not perceiving. The verse marks the solemn ground of the turning to the Gentiles (v.28): the Jews' judicial blindness. The prophecy of judicial blindness, the hearing without understanding, displays the solemn mystery of the Jews' rejection — God's judgment and their own self-hardening together — and the inexcusableness of those who hear the clear word of the gospel yet refuse to perceive. The verse sets up the full description of the hardened heart (v.27).
27:
"For the heart of this people is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes have they closed; lest they should see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them." The witnesses note the description of the hardened heart (Isaiah 6:10). On "waxed gross," the grammatical voices note the spiritual dullness. Gill and others note the heart "waxed fat" — stupefied with carnal things, hardened against spiritual truth.

On "their eyes have they closed," the witnesses note the wilful blindness. The people themselves closed their eyes and stopped their ears, lest they should see, hear, understand, and be converted — a deliberate refusal of the truth.

On "lest... I should heal them," the witnesses note the tragic end. The closing of eyes and ears prevents conversion and healing; the people, by their own hardening, cut themselves off from God's mercy. The verse displays the hardened heart and its tragic consequence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heart of the people waxed gross (stupefied, hardened against spiritual truth, Gill), their ears dull, their eyes closed — a wilful, deliberate refusal, lest they should see, hear, understand, be converted, and healed. The verse displays the hardened heart and its tragic consequence: the people, by their own hardening, cut off from God's mercy. The verse marks the depth of the judicial blindness: heart, ears, and eyes all closed. The verse displays the wilful refusal: the people themselves closing their eyes. The verse displays the tragic end: no conversion, no healing, by their own hardening. The verse displays the mercy implied: God would heal them if they would turn. The description of the hardened heart displays both the depth of the people's blindness and the mercy they refused. The verse displays the responsibility of the unbelieving: closing their own eyes. The verse displays the longing of God: that they might turn and be healed. The verse displays the tragedy: the people forfeiting mercy by their hardening. The hardened heart, the closed eyes, the forfeited healing, display the solemn consequence of resisting the gospel — the people, by their own wilful blindness, cutting themselves off from the conversion and healing God would freely give. The verse displays the heart of the warning: the danger of hardening against the truth. The verse marks the ground of the turning to the Gentiles (v.28): the Jews' self-imposed blindness leaving them without the healing they refused. The description of the hardened heart displays the tragic forfeiture of God's mercy by those who close their own eyes to the gospel.
28:
"Be it known therefore unto you, that the salvation of God is sent unto the Gentiles, and that they will hear it." The witnesses note the climactic turning to the Gentiles. On "the salvation of God is sent unto the Gentiles," the grammatical voices note the solemn announcement. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note Paul, the Jews having rejected the gospel, declares (as at Antioch, 13:46, and Corinth, 18:6) that the salvation of God is now sent to the Gentiles — the great theme of Acts reaching its climax.

On "they will hear it," the witnesses note the contrast. Robertson notes the emphatic "they" — the Gentiles, opposed to the rejecting Jews, will hear and receive. Barnes notes Paul's unwavering faith: if the gospel is rejected by one, he offers it to another.

The Reformed Calvin notes the order: salvation sent to the Gentiles in the second place, after the Jews rejected it; and not to all Gentiles indiscriminately, but the believing Gentiles set against the unbelieving Jews, to provoke Israel to jealousy (Romans 11:11; Deuteronomy 32:21). The verse displays the climactic turning to the Gentiles.

The witnesses agree: Paul, the Jews having rejected the gospel, solemnly declares (as at Antioch, 13:46, and Corinth, 18:6) that the salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles, and they will hear it — the emphatic "they" contrasting the receptive Gentiles with the rejecting Jews (Robertson), Paul's unwavering faith offering the gospel where it would be received (Barnes). Calvin notes the order: salvation sent to the Gentiles in the second place, after the Jews rejected it, not indiscriminately but to provoke Israel to jealousy (Romans 11:11). The verse displays the climactic turning to the Gentiles — the great theme of Acts reaching its climax. The verse marks the consummation of the book's movement: from Jerusalem to the Gentiles, from the Jews to the nations. The verse displays the gospel's universal reach: the salvation of God sent to the Gentiles. The verse displays the contrast: the Jews rejecting, the Gentiles receiving. The verse displays Paul's unwavering faith: the gospel offered where it would be heard. The climactic turning to the Gentiles displays the great theme of Acts: the salvation of God going forth to all nations. The verse displays the providence: the Jews' rejection opening the door to the Gentiles. The verse displays the hope for Israel (Calvin): the Gentiles' faith provoking the Jews to jealousy. The verse marks the theological climax of Acts: the gospel, rejected by the Jews, sent to the Gentiles who will hear. The climactic turning to the Gentiles, the salvation of God going forth to the nations, displays the consummation of the book of Acts and the universal reach of the gospel — sent to the Gentiles, who will hear and receive the salvation the Jews refused. The verse displays the great movement of redemptive history reaching its appointed end.
29:
"And when he had said these words, the Jews departed, and had great reasoning among themselves." The witnesses note the Jews' departure and dispute — and the textual question. On the verse itself, the grammatical voices note the divided debate. Barnes notes the believing party (v.24) reasoned warmly with the unbelieving; Clarke notes such contentions about the truth end in the triumph of the gospel.

On the text, the witnesses note the doubt. Barnes, Ellicott, and others note the whole verse is wanting in many early manuscripts and versions; it may be a later insertion (or a revised reading) to smooth the transition from v.28 to v.30. It is regarded by many as not original.

The Reformed Calvin notes the effect: the wicked, nettled by the prophecy, reasoned among themselves; yet some so embraced Paul's words as to defend them stoutly — for if we will have peace with God, we must war with those who contemn him. The verse displays the Jews' departure and dispute (where attested).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews departed and reasoned warmly among themselves — the believing party contending with the unbelieving (Barnes, Clarke) — though the whole verse is textually doubtful, wanting in many early manuscripts and likely a later insertion to ease the transition (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin notes the effect: the wicked, nettled by the prophecy, disputed; yet some embraced Paul's words and defended them. The verse displays the Jews' departure and dispute (where attested). The verse marks the divided reasoning: belief contending with unbelief. The verse displays the textual question: the verse absent from many witnesses. The verse displays the perennial effect of the gospel: contention ending in the truth's advance (Clarke). The verse displays the division: some defending Paul's words, some resisting. The Jews' departure and dispute (textually doubtful) display the divided reception of the gospel and the contention it stirred. The verse displays the providence: even the dispute advancing the truth. The verse displays the honest note of the textual question: the verse not in the earliest witnesses. The verse marks the transition to the close of Acts (vv.30-31): the Jews departed, Paul continuing his ministry. The Jews' departure and dispute, where the verse is attested, display the dividing power of the gospel; and the textual question displays the careful transmission of the sacred text. The verse, whether original or a later gloss, marks the close of Paul's formal reasoning with the Roman Jews and the turn to his continued, unhindered ministry to all who came.
30:
"And Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired house, and received all that came in unto him," The witnesses note Paul's two years of ministry at Rome. On "two whole years," the grammatical voices note the period. Barnes and others note Paul dwelt two years in his own hired lodging (with Luke, Aristarchus, and others), guarded by a soldier — a settled, fruitful imprisonment.

On "received all that came," the witnesses note the open ministry. Paul welcomed all who came to him — Jews and Gentiles alike — preaching and teaching freely. The puritan Henry and others note the providence: even a prisoner, Paul had a wide and open door.

The witnesses note the fruit of these two years: during this time Paul wrote several epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) and converted many, even in Caesar's household (Philippians 1:12-14; 4:22). The verse displays Paul's settled, fruitful ministry at Rome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired lodging (with companions, guarded by a soldier, Barnes) and received all who came — Jews and Gentiles alike — a settled, open, fruitful ministry. Henry notes the providence: even a prisoner, Paul had a wide and open door. The witnesses note the fruit: during these two years Paul wrote several epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) and converted many, even in Caesar's household (Philippians 1:12-14; 4:22). The verse displays Paul's settled, fruitful ministry at Rome. The verse marks the open door: receiving all who came. The verse displays the providence: the prisoner with a wide ministry. The verse displays the fruit: epistles written, souls converted. The verse displays the breadth: Jews and Gentiles welcomed. Paul's two years of open ministry display the providence turning imprisonment into opportunity. The verse displays the apostle's tireless labor: even in bonds, receiving all and preaching. The verse displays the spread of the gospel: from Paul's hired house through Rome and beyond (the epistles). The verse marks the fruitful close of Paul's recorded ministry: two years of open witness at the heart of the empire. Paul's two years of open, fruitful ministry display the providence of God turning the apostle's bonds into the gospel's advance — receiving all, preaching to Jews and Gentiles, writing the epistles that would feed the church through the ages. The verse displays the unhindered witness of the prisoner-apostle at Rome, the capital of the world. The verse sets up the final summary (v.31): preaching the kingdom with all boldness, no man forbidding him.
31:
"Preaching the kingdom of God, and teaching those things which concern the Lord Jesus Christ, with all confidence, no man forbidding him." The witnesses note the triumphant, unhindered close of Acts. On "preaching the kingdom of God, and teaching... the Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the two-fold subject. Clarke and Barnes note Paul preached the kingdom of God (its spiritual nature) and the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ — his person as Lord, Jesus, and Christ; the sum of the gospel.

On "with all confidence" (boldness), the witnesses note the freedom. Barnes and Clarke note Paul preached openly and boldly (parresia, full liberty of speech) — fearlessly, with the fullest toleration.

On "no man forbidding him," the witnesses note the open door and the abrupt close. Ellicott notes the Roman empire, even under Nero, was as yet tolerant; the history closes abruptly — perhaps Luke intended to continue, or wrote at the end of the two years, or Theophilus already knew the sequel. Barnes notes the fitness: the gospel conducted to the capital of the world, proclaimed unhindered. The verse displays the triumphant close of Acts.

The witnesses agree: Paul, at the close of Acts, preached the kingdom of God and taught the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ (the sum of the gospel, Clarke, Barnes), with all confidence (parresia, full boldness and liberty of speech) and no man forbidding him — the Roman empire as yet tolerant (Ellicott), the gospel proclaimed unhindered at the capital of the world (Barnes). Ellicott notes the abrupt close: perhaps Luke intended to continue, or wrote at the end of the two years, or Theophilus knew the sequel. The verse displays the triumphant, unhindered close of Acts. The verse marks the climactic ending: the gospel preached freely at Rome. The verse displays the two-fold subject: the kingdom of God and the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the boldness: Paul preaching with full liberty. The verse displays the open door: no man forbidding him. The verse displays the fitness of the close: the gospel conducted to the world's capital, proclaimed without hindrance. The triumphant close of Acts displays the unstoppable advance of the gospel: from Jerusalem to Rome, from the Jews to the Gentiles, preached at last in the imperial city with all boldness, no man forbidding. The verse displays the great theme fulfilled: the word of God, bound by no chains, reaching the heart of the empire. The verse marks the open-ended close: the gospel still advancing, the witness unhindered. The triumphant, unhindered close of Acts — the kingdom of God preached at Rome with all boldness, no man forbidding — displays the victory of the gospel and the faithfulness of God, who brought his servant to Rome and opened the door for the word to go forth freely to the ends of the earth. The verse fittingly closes the book of the gospel's advance with the unhindered proclamation of Christ at the capital of the world.
